edges.description: KRT1 variation disrupts the keratinocyte cytoskeletal program that supports palmoplantar epidermal differentiation.
Abnormal palmoplantar keratinization directly produces diffuse thickening of the palms and soles.
The hyperkeratosis occurs without epidermolysis on histology.
Disordered cornification contributes to palmoplantar scaling.
Abnormal keratinization and cornification produce diffuse dry skin beyond the core palmoplantar thickening.
Ectopic hyperkeratosis at the umbilicus produces the abnormal umbilicus morphology phenotype.
Hyperkeratotic lesions on fingers manifest as knuckle pad-like keratoses.
Disordered epidermal cornification can extend beyond the palmoplantar surface and produce scaling skin.
Disordered palmoplantar keratinization can be accompanied by blistering, but the exact intervening fragility mechanism is not specified in the structured evidence.
Epidermal hyperkeratosis and superficial inflammatory changes can be accompanied by erythema, but the immediate pathway is not resolved.
KRT1-related barrier and keratinization disturbance is associated with occasional atopic dermatitis, but the exact inflammatory intermediates are not established in the available evidence.
KRT1-related keratinization disturbance includes nail involvement, though the exact route to concave nails is not defined.
The inherited keratoderma is frequently complicated by fungal infection, including onychomycosis.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hyperkeratotic skin and nail changes predispose to recurrent fungal infection.

edges.description: Tendon thickening is reported in KRT1-related diffuse NEPPK, but the immediate mechanism linking the keratinocyte defect to tendon involvement is not specified.
Achilles tendon thickening is reported as a localized tendon phenotype, but the exact intermediate mechanism is not specified.
Finger hyperkeratosis, knuckle pad-like lesions, or other periarticular changes may reduce interphalangeal motion, but the precise intermediate mechanism is unresolved.
edges.target: Decreased movement range in interphalangeal joints

edges.description: Nonepidermolytic histology distinguishes the hyperkeratosis from epidermolytic keratinopathies.

evidence.reference_title: KRT1-related diffuse nonepidermolytic keratoderma (Orphanet structured-database record)

nodes.description: Disease-causing germline KRT1 variants alter keratin 1, a type II keratin expressed in suprabasal epidermal keratinocytes. In diffuse NEPPK pedigrees, KRT1 variation segregates with diffuse palmoplantar keratoderma and points to a keratin filament defect distinct from KRT9-related epidermolytic PPK.

nodes.description: The KRT1-related keratinocyte defect produces abnormal palmoplantar keratinization and diffuse hyperkeratosis while preserving nonepidermolytic histology. This separates the entity from epidermolytic palmoplantar keratoderma and supports the diffuse palmoplantar hyperkeratosis phenotype.

nodes.description: Oral retinoids are a systemic option for severe hereditary palmoplantar keratoderma, but benefit varies by PPK subtype and tolerability can limit use.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of hereditary palmoplantar keratoderma: a review by analysis of the literature.
evidence.snippet: Oral retinoids appear to be the most efficient treatment, but not in all PPK forms, and with variable tolerance.

nodes.description: Lesional skin biopsy shows orthokeratotic hyperkeratosis, acanthosis, hypergranulosis, and mild upper-dermal lymphocytic infiltrates without epidermolysis, matching the nonepidermolytic clinical designation.

evidence.reference_title: KRT1-related diffuse nonepidermolytic keratoderma (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: Histological examination of lesions reveals orthokeratotic hyperkeratosis, acanthosis, hypergranulosis, and mild lymphocyte infiltrations in the upper dermis with no evidence of epidermolysis.

nodes.description: Topical emollients, keratolytics, topical retinoids, and topical steroids are used symptomatically to soften hyperkeratosis, reduce scale, and relieve pain or functional impairment. These treatments are not curative.
nodes.id: Topical emollient and keratolytic symptom therapy

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of hereditary palmoplantar keratoderma: a review by analysis of the literature.
evidence.snippet: Topical (emollients, keratolytics, retinoids, steroids) and systemic treatments (mostly different retinoids), often combined, are used to relieve symptoms.
